one_line_takeaway: PPIA shows that a fully black-box, query-agnostic, purely physical typographic prompt injection -- with the target model's own name as a trigger, an offline cross-entropy-based prompt selector, and CLIP-attention-guided placement -- reaches 70-98% attack success across 10 commercial and open-source LVLMs in both simulation and real-world robotic navigation, and that only aggressive (usability-costly) OCR-based text masking meaningfully defends against it.
key_finding: In simulation (Table II), PPIA's average ASR exceeds 70% in most model/task/environment combinations, e.g., gpt-4-turbo reaches 98% ASR on QA/NAV in Embodied City and Habitat, gemini-1-p2 reaches 98% across nearly all tasks in both simulators; the notable outlier is claude-3-5-haiku, where ASR is much lower (as low as 16-35%), which the authors attribute to weaker visual-text interpretation relative to claude-3-5-sonnet-latest. In the comparative evaluation (Figure 5) against typographic-attack baselines, PPIA achieves the highest ASR and 'substantially stronger cross-prompt transferability' particularly in the unknown-user-query setting, where prompt-dependent baselines degrade sharply. The optimal-prompt-selection stage is empirically validated (Figure 6): prompts with lower cross-entropy scores tend to have higher ASR across the 100 generated candidates evaluated across 10 models. The optimal-location-searching stage is validated by an ASR map that correlates with the spatiotemporal CLIP attention map (Figure 7) and an ablation placing the same container/prompt at three different locations, yielding ASR of 70%, 82%, and 87% corresponding to increasing attention weight (Figure 17). Physical-world deployment (Table IV) achieves ASR ranging from 80% (llama3.2-90b-vi) to 98% (gemini-1-p2) on the Navigation task across 6 models. Robustness results (Table III, simulation) show PPIA tolerates text rotation up to 20 degrees and viewpoint angles up to 60 degrees with limited degradation, but degrades sharply as text/container size shrinks below 5% of image area (e.g., claude-3-5-sl ASR drops from 72% at size=13% to 0% at size=5%/3%) or rotation exceeds 45 degrees. Physical robustness (Table V) shows ASR of 55-97% even at night, and degradation with increasing camera-to-container distance (e.g., gpt-4o ASR falls from 93% at 1m to 36% at 5m) and viewing angle beyond 45-60 degrees. A language-impact test (Table VI) shows English prompts substantially outperform Chinese/French/Spanish prompts (e.g., llama3.2-90b-vi: 92% English vs 12-18% other languages), attributed to English-dominant LLM training data. Defense evaluation (Figure 18, discussion section) shows strict OCR-based text masking provides the strongest mitigation but at high usability cost (removing benign task-relevant text), while looser prompt-based or OCR-based defenses remain vulnerable because the attack does not rely on explicit malicious keywords.
core_contribution: The paper proposes PPIA (Physical Prompt Injection Attack), described as the first black-box, query-agnostic physical-world prompt injection attack against Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). Unlike prior visual/typographic prompt injection work (TypoD, SGTA, SceneTAP), which the paper places in Related Work and explicitly distinguishes from its own method, PPIA requires no access to the model, its inputs/inference pipeline, or the user's query at attack-generation time, and no interactive probing of the deployed system. PPIA has two core stages, built on top of an LLM-generated candidate pool of malicious textual prompts: (1) Optimal Prompt Selection - candidate prompts are virtually deployed into environment images via random container placement/rotation/scaling, then scored offline using a cross-entropy-based 'recognizability' metric computed with a pretrained multimodal OCR-capable model (Llama-3.2-11B-Vision), selecting the prompt with lowest cross-entropy (highest expected recognizability); (2) Optimal Location Searching - CLIP (as a black-box proxy for the target LVLM's ViT-based visual encoder) is used to compute spatiotemporal attention maps over a sequence of frames, and the region with highest attention (subject to accessibility/legality constraints) is chosen as the deployment location for a physical container (e.g., a paper bag, poster, book, or screen) carrying the printed malicious prompt. The paper also reports a small motivating 'identity sensitivity' experiment (Fig. 1) showing LVLMs are more likely to obey injected commands that reference their assumed name/identity (e.g., 'Nova'), which the authors use to justify embedding the target model's public name as a trigger phrase in generated prompts.
threat_model: Attacker has NO access to the target LVLM's architecture, parameters, training data, internal activations, textual input interface, or the user's query (neither exact text nor semantic intent) at attack-generation time -- this is the 'unknown-user-query setting' the paper focuses on (Section III). The attacker cannot query or probe the deployed system interactively. The attacker CAN: (1) freely choose and place an environment-appropriate physical container (e.g., poster, sign, paper bag, book, screen) carrying printed malicious text within the field of view of the target LVLM-based system's camera; (2) obtain minimal public information such as the model/system name. Formally, the LVLM is modeled as y = f_theta(x, p) with visual observation x, user prompt p, output y; the attacker composes a modified observation x' = x (+) t via visual insertion of a textual prompt t (not pixel-level perturbation), and seeks to maximize a task-dependent semantic objective E_p[L(f_theta(x'+t, p), y*)] over the choice of t, without knowledge of p. The scenario given is an LVLM-driven navigation/assistant task (e.g., 'How do I get to KFC?') where a malicious sign or object in the environment hijacks the perception output.
limitations: The authors explicitly state two limitations (Section VII, Discussion): (1) physical performance may be degraded by low camera resolution on the intelligent-vehicle platform, weakening visibility of embedded prompts; (2) physical evaluations were conducted in relatively controlled environments (office workspace, single-floor building, one outdoor road segment) that may not capture the full variability of dynamic real-world scenes. Beyond the authors' own list: the attack's target-word-match ASR metric (any target string appearing in output counts as success) does not measure downstream task-impact severity; the cross-entropy-based recognizability proxy relies on a single OCR-capable surrogate model (Llama-3.2-11B-Vision) and CLIP attention as a black-box proxy for the target's own attention, which may not transfer perfectly to all target LVLMs (the paper does not report calibration/transfer error between the CLIP proxy attention and each target model's actual attention); English-language dependence sharply limits attack effectiveness in non-English deployments; and the identity-sensitivity motivating experiment (Fig. 1) is a small, qualitative 4-model demo rather than a systematically validated mechanism.

datasets_used: Manually designed pool of 100+ user queries for QA/Task Planning/Navigation tasks
LLM-generated pool of N=100 candidate malicious prompts per attack goal
Real-world physical test environments: office workspace, single-floor building, outdoor road-level environment (unmanned ground vehicle)
quantitative_results: Simulation ASR up to 98% across 10 LVLMs, 3 tasks (QA/TP/NAV), 2 simulators (Table II)
claude-3-5-haiku is the weakest target, ASR as low as 16-35% depending on task/simulator
Physical-world NAV task ASR: gpt-4o 95%, gpt-4-turbo 92%, gemini-1-p2 98%, gemini-1-fl 98%, claude-3-5-sl 98%, llama3.2-90b-vi 80% (Table IV)
Text size robustness: ASR drops to 0% for several models (e.g., claude-3-5-sl, gemini-1-p2) when container occupies only 3-5% of image area, vs 72-98% at 13% (Table III)
Text rotation robustness: ASR retained through 20 degrees, drops significantly at 45 degrees (Table III)
Viewpoint robustness: PPIA remains robust up to 60 degrees viewing angle (Table III)
Location-search ablation: same prompt/container at 3 real-world locations yields ASR 70%, 82%, 87% correlating with attention weight (Fig. 17)
Night-time physical ASR ranges 55-97% across models (Table V)
Distance robustness: gpt-4o ASR falls from 93% at 1m to 36% at 5m (Table V)
Language impact: llama3.2-90b-vi ASR 92% (English) vs 12-18% (Chinese/French/Spanish) (Table VI); gpt-4-turbo 98% (English) vs 19-24% (other languages)
Strict OCR-based masking is the strongest evaluated defense but degrades benign task utility (Fig. 18, qualitative)
baselines_compared: Multi-image typographic attacks (Wang, Zhao & Larson, NAACL 2025)
SceneTAP -- scene-coherent typographic adversarial planner (Cao et al., CVPR 2025)
SGTA -- self-generated typographic attacks (Qraitem et al., arXiv 2024)
